question.title: You are being invited to participate in a research study titled "Strategy Learning". This study is being done by [anonymised].
question.explanation:
The purpose of this research study is to test how well humans can learn strategies in specific problem solving scenarios. If you agree to take part in this study, you will be asked to complete an online survey/questionnaire.

This survey/questionnaire will take you approximately 45 minutes to complete. At some point there is a hint that you may take a short break if needed. However, please ensure that you still complete the questionnaire in one sitting, that is do not interrupt the study for more than around 10 minutes.

You may not directly benefit from this research; however, we hope that your participation in the study may give us insight into human strategy learning on the basis of which we aim to build better systems for supporting problem solving in human-AI teams based on tailored explanations.

We believe there are no known risks associated with this research study; however, as with any online related activity the risk of a breach of confidentiality is always possible. To the best of our ability your answers in this study will remain confidential. We will minimize any risks by never storing any data with regards to your person in the first place.

Your participation in this study is completely voluntary and you can withdraw at any time.

The tasks presented in this study are really complex and require concentration. We recommend to be alone in the room and facilitate a quiet atmosphere.

By clicking “I agree” below you are indicating that you are at least 18 years old, have read and understood this consent form and agree to participate in this research study. Please print a copy of this page for your records.

item.text: No, I do not agree (do not participate in this study)

question.title: You have answered all questions, thank you for your time! Can we use your data in anonymous form for scientific purposes?

item.text: Yes, I have correctly answered all questions. My data can be used for the analyses.
No, I wanted to "just look", take part repeatedly, or do not want my data to be used for analyses.

question.title: Please be honest: Did you like participating this study? Please answer truely - your answer has no consequences for you!
Have you carried out all the tasks as described in the respective instructions? Please reply also honestly - this answer has no consequences for you!

item.text: I have completed all the tasks as requested in the instructions.
I __sometimes__ clicked something because I was unmotivated or simply did not know.
I have __often__ clicked something, so I am quickly done.

question.title: Could you complete the questionnaire in one without being distracted? Please reply honestly.

item.text: Yes, I have given the study full attention and gone through it in one.
I was distracted by my environment (people, noises, etc.) __once__.
I was distracted by my environment (people, noises, etc.) __several times__.

question.title: Do you have a guess as to what the investigation was about?
question.explanation: You can write down your thoughts briefly in the text field. If you have no idea, just leave the field blank.

question.lead: Thank you for considering participating in our study! You may close the browser window now.

question.title: What is your year of birth?
Finally, we'd like to ask you for some details about yourself.
question.lead: Your are:

question.title: This question is intended to be attached to the question "Gender (to be combined)". Please also see the example questionnaire.
Which country are you currently living in?
Which is the country, you're currently living?
question.explanation: <!-- Based in subsequent country lists (state 06/2013) http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_numeric http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_and_their_capitals_in_native_languages -->

question.title: What are the first two digits of your postal code?
question.explanation: If you're not from Germany, please specify your international license plate country code.

item.text: My zip code starts with the digits

question.title: What is the highest level of education you have completed?

item.text: Vocational secondary certification (completion of specialized secondary school/college)
A-levels/International Baccalaureate, subject-related higher education entrance qualification

question.title: Education
question.explanation: Please select the highest level of qualification you have obtained.

item.text: High school diploma/Intermediate/General Certificate of Secondary Education, secondary school-leaving certificate or equivalent
Vocational baccalaureate diploma, vocational secondary certification
Vocational university/university of applied sciences/university degree

question.title: What professional qualifications do you have?

item.text: Master/technical certification or equivalent vocational qualification
Vocational university/university of applied sciences degree

residualOptions.li: I am still in school or training

question.title: What is your monthly net income?
question.explanation: Net income is defined as your total income after tax and social security deductions.

item.text: I do not have a personal income
I do not wish to answer

question.title: What is your monthly net income?
question.explanation: Net income is defined as your total income after tax and social security deductions.

item.text: I do not wish to answer

question.title: Would you like to comment this questionnaire, or would you like to add information for us to better understand your answers?
question.explanation: Do you think, this questionnaire needs improvement? Did you feel some question were unclear, or did you feel unpleasent answering specific questions? Please leave us notes.

question.title: Domain Introduction: Identifying Faults in Circuits
question.explanation: Please take your time to familiarize yourself with the domain. Make sure you understand all the components before you continue to the next page.
question.lead:
Your task is to perform tests to detect faults at connections in electrical circuits. Each circuit has one or more sources of energy (battery), lightbulbs (which should always be on), and gates (which are connections between cables). If a gate is faulty, it does not transfer energy, and the lightbulbs connected to it turn off. To find the faulty gate, you can add an additional power source at a test point, which potentially makes the lightbulb turn on again, giving you information about the location of the fault in the circuit. Here is a simple illustration:

These are simplified circuits in that we can always assume that the circuit is closed after the lightbulb and the fault lies "on the path to the lightbulb". Here is a summary of the components of the circuits:

  • Battery: The battery is the "source" of the current going through the circuit.
  • Cable: The arrows are cables which transfer current. Note that current is only transfered in the direction of the arrows!
  • Gate: The shapes labeled with numbers (in the example above "1") are gates which connect the current from the incoming arrow to the outgoing arrow. They may be faulty, in that they do not output the current, although they receive current from all input cables.
  • Lightbulb: The lightbulb turns on if it receives power from all input cables.
  • Test point: Test points are cables labeled with letters (in the example above "A"). You can perform a test by supplying power to such a cable, effectively ignoring all prior gates.

question.title: Domain Introduction (ctd.)
question.explanation: Please take your time to familiarize yourself with the domain. Make sure you understand all the components before you continue to the test items.
question.lead:
Both the gate and the lightbulb only work if they receive current from all connected input cables (similar to an AND gate). Consider these examples:

Gate 1 in the left example is only active if it receives current from both input cables. Similarly, the lightbulb in the right example must receive current from both input cables to turn on.

question.title: Worked Example
question.lead:
Each circuit you will see in this study has exactly one faulty gate. Your task is to choose the first test to perform so that the fault can be found with the least tests possible, regardless of the result of the test. Here is a worked out example:

In this scenario, two tests would be equally viable: C and D. Consider this for test C: If the lightbulb turns on, the fault must be in the top part, otherwise the bottom part. In either case, the next test will certainly reveal the fault, as there are only two options left. For test D the same principle applies, but with the top and bottom switched.

The circuits you will see in this study are more complex than this one and are randomly ordered. This means that some early circuits may be much more complex than ones you will encounter later. To keep the study short, we only ask for the first test to perform for each given circuit. In total, there are 14 circuits in this study.

The circuits you will see in this study are more complex than this one and increase in complexity towards the end. To keep the study short, we only ask for the first test to perform for each given circuit. In total, there are 14 circuits in this study.

question.title: Electrical Circuits Refresher
question.explanation: The first section on this page is a refresher and contains only information which has been presented previously in this study. The second section, however, shows a worked example of the tasks on the following pages
question.lead:
Your task is to perform tests to detect faults at connections in electrical circuits. Each circuit has one or more sources of energy (battery), lightbulbs (which should always be on), and gates (which are connections between cables). If a gate is faulty, it does not transfer energy, and the lightbulbs connected to it turn off. To find the faulty gate, you can add an additional power source at a test point, which potentially makes the lightbulb turn on again, giving you information about the location of the fault in the circuit. Here is a simple illustration:

These are simplified circuits in that we can always assume that the circuit is closed after the lightbulb and the fault lies "on the path to the lightbulb". Here is a summary of the components of the circuits:

  • Battery: The battery is the "source" of the current going through the circuit.
  • Cable: The arrows are cables which transfer current. Note that current is only transfered in the direction of the arrows!
  • Gate: The shapes labeled with numbers (in the example above "1") are gates which connect the current from the incoming arrow to the outgoing arrow. They may be faulty, in that they do not output the current, although they receive current from all input cables.
  • Lightbulb: The lightbulb turns on if it receives power from all input cables.
  • Test point: Test points are cables labeled with letters (in the example above "A"). You can perform a test by supplying power to such a cable, effectively ignoring all prior gates.

Both the gate and the lightbulb only work if they receive current from all connected input cables (similar to an AND gate). Consider these examples:

Gate 1 in the left example is only active if it receives current from both input cables. Similarly, the lightbulb in the right example must receive current from both input cables to turn on.

item.text: I have seen the worked example and know what I have to do in the next task.
I have seen the worked example and know what I have to do in the next task.
I have seen the worked example and know what I have to do in the next task.

question.title: Pipe Networks Introduction
question.explanation: Please familiarize yourself with this new problem scenario. The first section explains the notation and rules, the second is a worked out example.
question.lead:
The goal of this task is to find a clogged pipe segment in a pipe network. Each network has one or more sources of water (water tower), faucets (which should always have running water), and clog risks (labeled pipe segments). If a pipe segment is clogged, no water can flow through it, and the faucets connected to it no longer have running water. To find the clogged pipe segment, you can read out the water pressure at valves in the network, which gives you information about the location of the clogged pipe segment. Here is a simple illustration:

Pipe networks can have the following components:

  • Water Tower: The water tower is the source of water pressure in the network.
  • Pipe: Pipes transport water.
  • Clog Risks: Labeled wide sections of the pipes are clog risks.
  • Faucet: If there is enough incoming pressure, the faucet will provide water.
  • Valve: The valves can be used to check the incoming water pressure in the pipe below.

Note that wherever two pipes are joint together, both of them must be pressurized for the outgoing pressure to be considered sufficient, like this:

question.title: Worked Example
question.lead:
Each pipe network in this study has exactly one clogged pipe segment. Your task is to choose the first test to perform so that the clogged segment can be found with the least tests possible, regardless of the result of the test. Here is a worked out example:

In this scenario, two tests would be equally viable: F and G. Consider this for test G: If G shows sufficient pressure, the clogged segment must be somewhere in the bottom part of the network. Otherwise, it must be in the top part, or vice versa when choosing F instead. Depending on the result, the clog can be located with a maximum of either two (bottom) or three (top) more tests.

question.title: Search in Ordered Lists Introduction
question.explanation: Please familiarize yourself with this new problem scenario. The first section explains the notation and rules, the second is a worked out example.
question.lead:
The goal of this task is to find a number in an ordered list of numbers. Numbers at positions marked with "?" are unknown, but must always be less than or equal to known numbers on their right, and greater than or equal to known numbers on their left. To find the number in question, you can check the number at a position marked with "?", giving you more information about the position of the number you are looking for. Here is an example of such a list:

Lists can have the following components:

  • Known Numbers: Circles with a number in them are known to you, so you can use them as orientation.
  • Unknown Numbers: Circles with a questionmark have there content hidden. They also always contain a number, but you don't know which one, except that they are always greater than or equal to every known number to their left, and always less than or equal to every known number to their right.

You can always assume that the number you are looking for is contained in the list.

question.title: Worked Example
question.lead:
The goal is to find the number 9.

In this scenario, two checks would be equally viable: C and D. We know that the number 9 must be to the right of the known number 2. As an example, consider option C: If it shows either 9 or a number greater than 9, the problem is solved because the goal is either found, or there is only one possible position left. If it instead shows a number less than 9, then the next check will reveal the position by the same principle.

question.title: Faulty Circuit
question.explanation: Which test should you perform first to find the fault with the least tests possible?

question.title: Clogged Pipes
question.explanation: Which valve should you check to find the clogged pipe segment with the least checks possible?

question.title: Ordered Lists
question.explanation: Which unknown number should you check first to find the target with the least checks possible?

question.lead: Find the number 4.
Find the number 12.
Find the number 7.
Find the number 15.
Find the number 6.

question.title: Trace the Forward Path of Power
question.explanation: Which gates and lightbulbs is battery "A" connected to?

question.title: Determine Power Sources
question.explanation: Find all gates and batteries which are exclusively a source of power for lightbulb Y, that is they don't supply current to any other lightbulb than Y.

question.title: Divide the Gates Into Groups
question.explanation: The goal of performing a test is to divide the gates in the network into two groups: The ones which may be faulty, and the ones which we can be certain work fine. Remember: Test Points are cables labeled with capital letters. To perform a test, consider the following: When you perform a test, you add a power supply to the selected cable. If the lightbulb turns on after supplying power, the fault must be somewhere before the test cable, as the network seems to work fine with power supplied at a point in the middle. If the lightbulb still doesn't turn on after supplying power, the fault must be after the test point. Since we also know that there is only one fault, this means all previous nodes work fine.
question.lead: Based on a test at point C, divide the gates into two groups: the ones which may be faulty and the ones which we can be certain work fine.

question.title: Divide the Gates Into Groups
question.explanation: These are the same instructions as on the previous page, in case you need to double check them. The goal of performing a test is to divide the gates in the network into two groups: The ones which may be faulty, and the ones which we can be certain work fine. Remember: Test Points are cables labeled with capital letters. To perform a test, consider the following: When you perform a test, you add a power supply to the selected cable. If the lightbulb turns on after supplying power, the fault must be somewhere before the test cable, as the network seems to work fine with power supplied at a point in the middle. If the lightbulb still doesn't turn on after supplying power, the fault must be after the test point. Since we also know that there is only one fault, this means all previous nodes work fine.
question.lead: Based on a test at point C, divide the gates into two groups: the ones which may be faulty and the ones which we can be certain work fine.

question.title: Decide which test is most appropriate to find the fault quickly and reliably.
question.lead:
Consider the following approaches to finding the fault in the same simple circuit. Familiarise yourself with the two strategies and think about which of the two is better suited for finding faults quickly and reliably.

Option 1:

Option 2:

question.title: Decide which test is most appropriate to find the fault quickly and reliably.
question.lead:
Consider the following approaches to finding the fault in the same simple circuit. Familiarise yourself with the two strategies and think about which of the two is better suited for finding faults quickly and reliably.

Option 1:

Option 2:

Choosing the Most Appropriate Test to Find a Fault

To decide which test is most appropriate for finding a fault quickly and reliably in an electrical circuit, we need to use several strategies:

Relevant Strategies

  1. Test Point Evaluator: Assesses different test points to determine which one provides the most useful information.
  2. Circuit Divider: Splits the circuit into sections based on where test points are located.
  3. Group Size Calculator: Counts how many gates fall into each group when a particular test is applied.
  4. Optimal Test Finder: Identifies which test creates the most balanced division of gates into groups.

How to Apply These Strategies

  1. First, identify all possible test points in the circuit (these are usually labeled with letters).
  2. For each test point, use the Circuit Divider to determine how it would split the gates into two groups:
    • Gates that might be faulty if this test point is used
    • Gates that would be confirmed as working properly
  3. Use the Group Size Calculator to count how many gates would be in each group for each potential test point.
  4. Apply the Optimal Test Finder to compare all possible test points and find the one that:
    • Creates the most balanced division (where the smaller group is as large as possible)
    • Minimizes the number of gates you would need to check after the test
    • Provides the clearest information about where the fault might be
  5. The test point that creates the most balanced division is usually the best choice because:
    • It eliminates the maximum number of possibilities with a single test
    • It divides the remaining possibilities into manageable groups
    • It helps you find the fault with the fewest additional tests

This approach is similar to a binary search strategy, where you try to eliminate half of the possibilities with each test. By choosing the test point that creates the most balanced division, you can find the fault in the circuit with the minimum number of tests, saving time and effort.

To solve this task, we use two main strategies:

  1. Path Checker (has_path): This checks if a gate can send power to a specific lightbulb's connection point. It works by:
    • Directly matching if the gate is the lightbulb's connection point itself
    • Following each outgoing arrow from the gate to see if any path leads to the lightbulb's connection point
  2. Exclusive Source Verifier (exclusive_source): This confirms a gate only powers one specific lightbulb (Y) by:
    • Using the Path Checker to verify it powers lightbulb Y
    • Checking it doesn't power any other lightbulb (using the Path Checker for all other lightbulbs)

The solution involves:

  1. Finding all gates that power lightbulb Y using the Path Checker
  2. For each candidate gate, using the Exclusive Source Verifier to confirm it doesn't power any other lightbulb
  3. Keeping only gates that pass both checks

This ensures we find gates that exclusively send power to lightbulb Y and nowhere else.

To solve the task of dividing gates into two groups based on a test at point C (one group that may be faulty and one that is certainly working), follow these steps:

Relevant Strategies

  1. Same circuit check:
    Confirm if a gate is in the same circuit as point C. This ensures we only consider gates affected by the test.
  2. Exclusively powers check:
    Determine if point C exclusively powers a gate. If true, the gate relies solely on C for power.
  3. Not exclusively powers check:
    Determine if point C does not exclusively power a gate. This means the gate gets power from other sources besides C.
  4. Partition operation:
    Split the list of gates into two groups using the above checks:
    • Group 1: Gates in the same circuit as C and exclusively powered by C.
    • Group 2: Gates in the same circuit as C but not exclusively powered by C.

Step-by-Step Application

  1. Identify relevant gates:
    Use the "same circuit check" to collect all gates in the same circuit as point C. Ignore gates outside this circuit.
  2. Apply the partition operation:
    • For Group 1 (certainly working if C passes the test):
      Use the "exclusively powers check" to find gates powered only by C. If C works correctly, these gates cannot be faulty.
    • For Group 2 (may be faulty):
      Use the "not exclusively powers check" to find gates powered by C and other sources. These gates could still be faulty even if C works.
  3. Result:
    The partition operation directly outputs the two groups:
    • Group 1: Gates that are certainly working (if C passes the test).
    • Group 2: Gates that may be faulty (regardless of C’s test outcome).

Key Insight

The test at point C helps isolate responsibility:

  • Gates exclusively powered by C depend entirely on C’s functionality.
  • Gates with multiple power sources might have issues beyond C.

This division pinpoints where faults could exist based on the test results.

To solve the task of dividing gates into two groups based on a test at point C (one group that may be faulty and one that is certainly working), follow these steps:

Relevant Strategies

  1. Same circuit check:
    Confirm if a gate is in the same circuit as point C. This ensures we only consider gates affected by the test.
  2. Exclusively powers check:
    Determine if point C exclusively powers a gate. If true, the gate relies solely on C for power.
  3. Not exclusively powers check:
    Determine if point C does not exclusively power a gate. This means the gate gets power from other sources besides C.
  4. Partition operation:
    Split the list of gates into two groups using the above checks:
    • Group 1: Gates in the same circuit as C and exclusively powered by C.
    • Group 2: Gates in the same circuit as C but not exclusively powered by C.

Step-by-Step Application

  1. Identify relevant gates:
    Use the "same circuit check" to collect all gates in the same circuit as point C. Ignore gates outside this circuit.
  2. Apply the partition operation:
    • For Group 1 (certainly working if C passes the test):
      Use the "exclusively powers check" to find gates powered only by C. If C works correctly, these gates cannot be faulty.
    • For Group 2 (may be faulty):
      Use the "not exclusively powers check" to find gates powered by C and other sources. These gates could still be faulty even if C works.
  3. Result:
    The partition operation directly outputs the two groups:
    • Group 1: Gates that are certainly working (if C passes the test).
    • Group 2: Gates that may be faulty (regardless of C’s test outcome).

Key Insight

The test at point C helps isolate responsibility:

  • Gates exclusively powered by C depend entirely on C’s functionality.
  • Gates with multiple power sources might have issues beyond C.

This division pinpoints where faults could exist based on the test results.

In the following, you will be asked to apply the knowledge you gained on the previous pages. If needed, take a break here.

For networks as seen on the previous pages, you will be asked to select only the first test to perform to find the fault in the network with the least tests possible.

Afterwards, you will see two different groups of tasks, which share some similarities with the network tasks handled so far. Detailed instructions and symbol explanations will be given before each of the tasks.

question.title: Please answer the following question.
question.explanation: Which Gate is NOT connected to lightbulb "X"?

item.text: All of them are connected to lightbulb "X"

question.title: Please answer the following question.
question.explanation: Consider the following network. Given the performed test (at point "A") and outcome (lightbulb turns on), are the gates 1 and 3 in the same group (i.e. both nodes are either known working or potentially faulty), or in different groups?

item.text: Yes, they are both in the same group.

question.title: Which of the two approaches required fewer tests to find the fault?

surveyLayout.name: sosci24
surveyLayout.title: SoSci Survey Standard 2024
surveyLayout.template:
%head%
%logo%
%header.image.img%
%progress%
%form.open%
%questionnaire%
%button.speak% %button.inverse% %button.zoom% %button.easy% %button.language% %button.break% %button.leave% %button.control%
%form.close%
%imprint%

attributes.specific.attr: color: white; border: 1px solid %color.8%; background-color: %color.4%; padding: 10px 20px; font-weight: 700;

Condition Randomizer Sociodemographics Learning Phase Intro Practice Part: Find Faults in Circuits

On next 9 pages the task of finding the fault in the circuit will be broken down into smaller parts. For each task, you will be presented with a specific circuit and task, which builds up to eventually deciding on the best test to perform.

]]> Please work through the practice tasks carefully. In case your answer has been incorrect, please pay special attention to the correct solution with the aim of understanding why the answer is correct. You always have the option to go back to previous pages if you want to look at your solution.

After the practice part, you will see additional tasks, where you have to decide which test to perform on your own.

]]> Explanation 1-2 Lightbulb Y is connected to gates 2, 3, 5, and 6 as well as the batteries B and C.
